episodes.title: Learning Brief — May 19, 2026

ai_news.title: Open Embeddings Model Gains 32K Context; Chinese Short-Form Video Goes All-In on AI Generation
points: IBM released Granite Embedding Multilingual R2, an open-source embedding model under Apache 2.0 with 32K context window and sub-100M parameters. It's positioned as the best-in-class retrieval quality at that scale, available on Hugging Face.

Chinese short-drama platforms have scaled AI content generation to industrialize production pipelines, creating narrative-driven video at volumes that would be economically unfeasible with traditional crews. Studios are using AI for scripting, asset generation, and editing to feed massive demand.
product_implications: If you're building RAG or semantic search into your product, this changes the math on build-versus-buy for embeddings. A 32K context window means you can embed longer documents without chunking, reducing retrieval complexity and improving accuracy on dense queries. The open license removes vendor lock-in risk and lets you fine-tune on proprietary data without sharing it. This is table stakes for any PM shipping search or knowledge retrieval in the next 6 months.

The Chinese short-drama model is a real-time case study in how AI commoditizes content production at scale. If you're in media, entertainment, or any narrative-driven product, this signals that cost-per-unit-content is collapsing for certain formats. Your competitive moat shifts from production capacity to taste, curation, and audience insight. Watch this space if you're considering user-generated or AI-assisted content features — the economics just changed.

pm_news.title: Aakash Gupta's AI Prototyping Bakeoff: What PMs Should Learn From Real Tool Selection
pm_news.content:
Aakash Gupta just published something that feels rare in the PM world right now — an actual, transparent bakeoff of AI prototyping tools. Not a concept piece, not a listicle. A real comparison with screenshots, workflows, and a downloadable playbook. He tested multiple tools, picked a winner, and added it to his bundle.

Here's why this matters beyond just "oh, new tools exist." This is a case study in how you should be evaluating emerging technology for your own product organization. Gupta didn't just read marketing copy or watch demos. He ran the tools through realistic workflows, compared outputs side by side, and made a deliberate choice based on what actually works in practice.

For you as a senior PM, this is instructive on two fronts. First, it's a model for how to pressure-test AI tools before you commit your team's time and muscle memory to them. Second, it shows what transparency looks like when you're making a tool decision — he's showing his work, which means you can learn from his criteria and potentially replicate his methodology for tools relevant to your own context.

The broader pattern here is important too. We're in a phase where AI tool maturity is moving fast — Anthropic shipped 74 releases in 52 days, according to another piece in the same publication. That velocity means your evaluation process needs to be continuous and grounded in actual use, not theoretical capability. You can't rely on launch announcements or feature parity tables anymore.

The takeaway: when you're selecting tools or evaluating AI capabilities for your product roadmap, build in a real testing phase with your actual workflows before you standardize on anything.

pm_learning.title: Get to the Core of the Thing
pm_learning.content:
Here's the thing that separates senior PMs from the rest: the willingness to stop being comfortable and actually get specific about what's broken.

Shreyas Doshi's core insight is this — most of us default to abstract language when we're thinking through problems. We say things like "user engagement is low" or "our onboarding needs work" or "we should focus on retention." And then we move on. But that's not thinking. That's just restating the problem in different words.

The move here is to push yourself — and your team — to the hard specifics. What exact behaviour isn't happening? Which users? In what sequence? What's the measurable gap between what's happening now and what should happen? When you force yourself to answer those questions, the actual problem usually becomes obvious. And it's almost never what you thought it was.

Let's make this concrete. Imagine you're working on a B2B SaaS product and you say "our trial-to-paid conversion is too low." That's abstract. Now push deeper. Is it that people aren't coming back after day three? Or they're coming back but not inviting teammates? Or they're inviting teammates but those teammates aren't doing anything? Those are completely different problems with completely different solutions. The first one might be a content problem. The second is a collaboration problem. The third is a permissions or role clarity problem. You can't solve anything until you know which one it is.

What that means in practice is this: when you're in a planning meeting or a discovery conversation, and someone says something vague, your job is to ask the uncomfortable follow-up question. Not to be difficult, but because the specificity is where the insight lives. "What do you mean by that?" "Which users specifically?" "How many?" "What's the exact moment they stop?" "How do you know?" These aren't rhetorical questions. You're genuinely trying to narrow the aperture until you can see the actual problem, not the shadow of it.

This is especially important as you move into senior or group PM roles, because you're now responsible for making sure your teams aren't spinning on vague problems. You're the one who has to say "we're not going to prioritize this until we can describe what's actually broken."

Here's your action for this week: pick one active project or problem your team is working on. Write down how you'd currently describe it in a sentence. Then ask yourself — or your team — the uncomfortable questions until you can rewrite that description with actual numbers, specific user segments, and measurable gaps. That's the difference between thinking and talking about thinking.
pm_learning.mental_model: You haven't understood the problem until you can describe it with specifics — not abstractions.

# Learning Brief — 2026-05-16

## What we covered
- **AI news:** Open Embeddings Model Gains 32K Context; Chinese Short-Form Video Goes All-In on AI Generation
- **PM news:** Aakash Gupta's AI Prototyping Bakeoff: What PMs Should Learn From Real Tool Selection
- **PM learning:** Get to the Core of the Thing

## Mental model

You haven't understood the problem until you can describe it with specifics — not abstractions.

## Summary

IBM released Granite Embedding Multilingual R2, an open-source embedding model under Apache 2.0 with 32K context window and sub-100M parameters. It's positioned as the best-in-class retrieval quality at that scale, available on Hugging Face. Chinese short-drama platforms have scaled AI content generation to industrialize production pipelines, creating narrative-driven video at volumes that would be economically unfeasible with traditional crews. Studios are using AI for scripting, asset generation, and editing to feed massive demand.

That velocity means your evaluation process needs to be continuous and grounded in actual use, not theoretical capability. You can't rely on launch announcements or feature parity tables anymore.

The takeaway: when you're selecting tools or evaluating AI capabilities for your product roadmap, build in a real testing phase with your actual workflows before you standardize on anything.
